titleInfo.title: Resetting the Diamond: Reflections on Kumārajīva’s Chinese Translation of the Vajracchedikā (‘ Diamond Sūtra ’)

mods.abstract: Gives an overview over surviving Sanskrit recensions of the Vaj, the earliest of which date about two centuries after Kumarajiva’s translation. Re-evaluates the notion that Kumarajiva has intentionally shortened the Sanskrit while translating. He did not do so systemmatically. Harrison explores three features of Kumarajiva’s translation style: 1. Glossing (for example, his use of 相, meaning not laksana, and ding 定 fixed, set, determined)2. Lexical flattening (most of all, in his use of xiang 相 for both, saṃjñā and lakṣaṇa) 3. a particular handling of the "signature formula" of the Vaj (affirmation-negation-affirmation). He shows that the signature formula is different from the Sanskrit. The particular signature formular that Kumarajiva choose dominates all translations of the sutra into English to this very day, as well as all attempts of a philosophical interpretation of it. Harrison concludes with a summaric characterization of what translation means, and in what respect a translation may be called a good or a bad one.

titleInfo.title: Journal of Historical and Philological Studies of China’s Western Regions
